paper_title: Multiplication in Multimodal LLMs: Computation with Text, Image, and Audio Inputs

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Multimodal LLMs can accurately perceive numerical content across modalities yet fail to perform exact multi-digit multiplication when the identical underlying arithmetic problem is presented as numerals, number words, images, or in audio form. Because existing benchmarks often lack systematically paired instances across modalities, it remains difficult to compare genuine arithmetic limits within and across model families. We therefore introduce a controlled multimodal multiplication benchmark that factorially varies digit length, digit sparsity, representation (e.g., numerals vs. number words), and modality (text, rendered images, audio), with paired instances from a reproducible generator. We also define arithmetic load, C, as the product of the total and non-zero digit count as a compact, mechanistically motivated proxy for operation count. Across evaluations, accuracy falls sharply as C grows, often nearing zero by C > 100. Indeed, C remains predictive of performance across modalities and models, with R-squared often > 0.5, nearing the value from more complex measures of arithmetic load that count the number of intermediate arithmetic steps. A separate perception-versus-computation decomposition shows that multimodal degradation is primarily computational rather than perceptual: on matched-perception checks, models are near-perfect (> 99%) across modalities, even when multiplication accuracy drops. Beyond measuring when models fail, we ask which procedures they are predisposed to follow. We introduce a forced-completion loss probe that scores heuristic-specific reasoning prefixes--including columnar multiplication, distributive decomposition, and rounding/compensation. Here, decomposition is favored in both text and vision modalities; heuristic-specific LoRA adapters produce near-orthogonal updates yet degrade accuracy, indicating the base model maintains a well-tuned internal router.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 0 2 ] . [ 1 3 0 2 8 1 . 4 0 6 2 : r Multiplication in Multimodal LLMs: Computation with Text, Image, and Audio Inputs* Samuel G. Balter University of Texas at Austin sgb2634@eid.utexas.edu Ethan Jerzak National University of Singapore (NUS) phiejj@nus.edu.sg Connor T. Jerzak University of Texas at Austin connor.jerzak@austin.utexas.edu

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Multimodal LLMs can accurately perceive numerical content across modalities yet fail to perform exact multi-digit multiplication when the identical underlying arithmetic problem is presented as numerals, number words, images, or in audio form. Because existing benchmarks often lack systematically paired instances across modalities, it remains difficult to compare genuine arithmetic limits within and across model families. We therefore introduce controlled multimodal multiplication benchmark that factorially varies digit length, digit sparsity, representation (e.g., numerals vs. number words), and modality (text, rendered images, and audio), with paired instances from reproducible generator. We also define arithmetic load, C, as the product of the TOTAL and NON-ZERO digit count as compact, mechanistically motivated proxy for operation count. Across evaluations, accuracy falls sharply as grows, often nearing zero by >100. Indeed, remains predictive of performance across modalities and models, with R2 > 0.5, nearing the value from more complex measures of arithmetic load that count the number of intermediate arithmetic steps. separate perception-versus-computation decomposition shows that multimodal degradation is primarily computational rather than perceptual: on matched perception checks, models are near-perfect (>99%) across modalities even when multiplication accuracy drops substantially. Beyond measuring when models fail, we ask which procedures they are predisposed to follow. We introduce style-controlled forcedcompletion loss probe that scores heuristicspecific reasoning prefixesincluding columnar multiplication, distributive decomposition, and rounding/compensation. Here, distributive decomposition is favored in both text and vision modalities; heuristic-specific LoRA adapters produce near-orthogonal updates yet degrade accuracy, indicating the base model maintains well-tuned internal router. *To appear in ACL Findings (2026). Large language models (LLMs) increasingly act as general-purpose assistants, and many real workflows require exact arithmetic. Unfortunately, arithmetic competence can be unpredictable: model that correctly computes 4736 may fail on nearby instance like 8967, despite both being two-digit multiplications. Recent evaluations confirm that multi-digit arithmetic can be brittle in LLMs (Mahendra et al., 2025); performance can vary with operand size and surface form in ways that are hard to anticipate (Yuan et al., 2023). Methods such as chain-of-thought prompting and math-focused pretraining improve performance on broader quantitative reasoning tasks (Wei et al., 2022; Lewkowycz et al., 2022), but they do not guarantee the reliable execution of the intermediate calculations those approaches rely on. Moreover, widely used benchmarks (e.g., GSM8K) can entangle language understanding, multi-step reasoning, and computation, making it difficult to attribute errors to arithmetic rather than to interpretation or planning (Cobbe et al., 2021). At the same time, foundation models are increasingly multimodal, with arithmetic questions often arriving as rendered equations in images (e.g., screenshots, slides, scanned homework) or audio traces instead of as text tokens (Alayrac et al., 2022; OpenAI, 2023). This issue becomes more important in agentic workflows: an agent may inspect screenshot, choose calculation path, call tools, and verify intermediate states. These steps, however, are most effective when the model captures the mathematical problem underlying the model presentation completely, instead of relying on superficial cues in answering. Recent benchmarks and training efforts in multimodal mathematical reasoning emphasize complex visual contexts and diverse problem types (Lu et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2024), but comparatively little work isolates elementary arithmetic under controlled problem structure and controlled modal format. This gap matters because representation and perception can amplify small computational weaknesses: the same multiplication might be easy as typed numerals but hard when embedded in noisy visual channel or reformulated as spoken number words. We address this gap with controlled experiments on multiplication, particularly sensitive probe because it requires carry propagation and long-range digit interactions. In the benchmark, we systematically vary (i) operand digit length, (ii) the number of non-zero digits, and (iii) modality (paired text, rendered images, and audio). Through this benchmark, we ask (1) How do digit length and algorithmic load (total digits non-zero digit count) determine accuracy? (2) How much does performance change when the same computation is presented across modalities and representations? (3) Which internal arithmetic heuristics do models prefer, how do these priors shift with operand cues and modality, and do they help explain failure patterns? Beyond accuracy, we will aim to characterize why failures occur by probing strategy preference. Human multiplication draws on multiple heuristicsincluding columnar and procedures, rounding/compensationwhose use depends on problem structure (Campbell, 2005). Inspired by probing methods for interpreting neural representations (Belinkov, 2022), we introduce forced-completion loss probe over heuristicspecific reasoning preambles, using only forward passes. Finally, using low-rank adapters (Hu et al., 2022), we analyze whether heuristic-specialized updates align in parameter space, providing geometric evidence about whether these heuristics correspond to distinct mechanisms. distributive decomposition, Our contributions are: Controlled multimodal benchmark. We introduce reproducible multiplication benchmark that factorially varies digit length, digit sparsity, representation, and modality under standardized evaluation across modalities. An interpretable difficulty measure. We show that arithmetic load (total digits non-zero digits) is strong predictor of accuracy and helps summarize degradation across modalities. Forced-completion loss-based strategy fingerprinting. We use forward-pass loss probe and find model-specific low-loss preferences, with margins shifting with operand cues and modality. Geometric evidence for distinct heuristics. Heuristic-specific LoRA adapters show separated effective update directions (cosine similarity of = BA), with stronger orthogonality in 235B than in 30B, suggesting distinct parameter subspaces.

sections.title: 2 Background
sections.content: 2.1 Arithmetic Reliability in LLMs To situate our controlled benchmark, we briefly summarize what prior work has established about exact arithmetic in LLMs. Although LLMs can produce fluent arithmetic explanations, controlled evaluations show that exact multi-digit computation can be brittle: accuracy drops quickly as digit length grows and carry interactions accumulate, while small changes in operand configuration can flip correctness even under deterministic decoding (Yuan et al., 2023). Prompting methods such as chain-of-thought can improve performance on many reasoning benchmarks by eliciting intermediate steps (Wei et al., 2022), and math-focused pretraining and verification-style training can further raise end-to-end scores on quantitative tasks (Lewkowycz et al., 2022; Cobbe et al., 2021). However, these approaches still rely on the model executing intermediate arithmetic correctly (Yuan et al., 2023). Moreover, widely used datasets such as GSM8K provide end-to-end correctness labels for word problems, which entangle language understanding, planning, and computation in ways that make arithmetic-specific failure modes difficult to isolate (Cobbe et al., 2021).

sections.title: 2.2 Representation and Modality Effects
sections.content: Beyond operand size and carry interactions, arithmetic success is also sensitive to how given computation is encoded and presented to the model. As noted, arithmetic questions increasingly arrive in diverse formatstyped numerals, number words, and multimodal inputs such as screenshots or rendered equationsas foundation models extend from text-only to vision-language interfaces (Alayrac et al., 2022; OpenAI, 2023). Recent multimodal benchmarks and instruction-tuned systems increasingly target broad mathematical reasoning in visual contexts (Lu et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2024), emphasizing heterogeneous problem types with 47 36 (same problem) What is 47 36? forty-seven times... 4736 =? p l m u MM-LLM e.g., Qwen3-VL 30B / 235B Arithmetic Load = dtotal dnon-zero 4 4 = audio Processor Forced-Completion Loss Probes (Cross-Entropy) OT Columnar 7.09/7.25 DD Decomposition 7.02/6. RC Rounding 7.08/6.78 lower indicates deeper alignment Cross-Modal Text Image preferences DD preferred in both LoRA Orthogonality OT DD RC ρ 0 (distinct subspaces) Key: DD preferred (text/image lowest-loss match, 30B: 68%/91%; 235B: 93%/100%) OT rarely lowest-loss match (text/image, 30B: 24%/16%; 235B: 0%/0%) Heuristics orthogonal (ρ 0) Figure 1: Overview of our arithmetic benchmark and heuristic fingerprinting methodology for multimodal LLMs. In the generator, the same multiplication problem (e.g., 47 36) is paired across numeric text, rendered numeric image, and audio. rich visual scenes (but with few, if any, evaluations that isolate elementary arithmetic under controlled modal variation). As result, it remains unclear how much multimodal degradation reflects perceptual/representation noise versus limitations in the underlying computation (Liu et al., 2024), and whether failures transfer systematically across modalities when the arithmetic itself is held fixed (Lu et al., 2023; Shi et al., 2024).

sections.title: Probing
sections.content: Even when the input format is held fixed, multiplication can be carried out via multiple procedures, and the choice of strategy affects both intermediate operation counts and characteristic error modes. Work in mathematical cognition emphasizes that multiplication can be performed using multiple solution strategies, with choice depending on operand structure, experience, and context (Campbell, 2005; Dehaene, 2011). We focus on three canonical heuristics that capture some of these distinct computational approaches: Columnar/long multiplication (OT): compute digit-by-digit partial productsintermediate products formed by multiplying one operand by single digit or place-value component of the other, such as 47 6 and 47 30 when solving 47 36with explicit carry propagation (the standard algorithm in contemporary primary education) (Campbell, 2005). OT can be attractive for unstructured or carry-heavy operands because it systematically computes every digit-by-digit partial product. Distributive decomposition (DD): rewrite an operand as sum and add partial products, e.g., 47 36 = 40 36 + 7 36 (Campbell, 2005; Dehaene, 2011). DD can be attractive when one operand has trailing zeros or other place-value structure because it converts dense multiplication into small number of simpler subproducts. Roundingcompensation (RC): round to convenient base and correct, e.g., 49 51 = (50 1)(50 + 1) = 502 1 (Campbell, 2005). RC can be attractive when operands are close to round numbers because it converts dense multiplication into simpler round-base operations plus small correction. These heuristics imply different computational burdens (number of partial products, carries, and adjustments) and characteristic failure modes when steps are omitted or misapplied (Campbell, 2005; Dehaene, 2011). To uncover which of these procedural paths given model naturally favors, we will draw on probing methods from the interpretability literature, which provide tools for testing the compatibility between specific computations and models internal representations (Belinkov, 2022).

sections.title: 3.1 Problem Design
sections.content: Building on the evidence above that arithmetic failures depend on both operand structure and surface form, we here design controlled multiplication benchmark that lets us vary these factors independently. We study multiplication in controlled setting by generating operand pairs from digit templates that vary the number and arrangement of non-zero digits. Concretely, we sample problems from the template family shown in Table 1, which spans fully dense numbers (e.g., VV, VVV) as well as structured sparsity through trailing zeros (e.g., V0, V00, VV0) and non-adjacent non-zeros (e.g., V0V). This design lets us vary arithmetic difficulty without changing the task format, yielding broad range of carry patterns. Template VV VVV V0, V00, VV0 V0V Description single random digit (19) two random digits three random digits trailing zeros (fewer operations needed) non-adjacent non-zero digits Table 1: Digit templates used to vary operand structure. To quantify difficulty with single interpretable scalar, we define arithmetic load as := Load(a, b) = dtotal dnon-zero, (1) where dtotal is the total number of digits across both operands and dnon-zero is the count of non-zero digits. For example, 47 36 has dtotal = 4 and dnon-zero = 4, yielding load = 16. This load score is simple, though imperfect, proxy for operation count (see B). In our sensitivity checks, it preserves nearly the same explanatory signal as more complex alternatives that explicitly count carry-propagation requirements within each multiplication problem. We therefore use as the main predictor and reserve carry-aware counts for validation.

sections.title: 3.2 Model Configuration
sections.content: With the problem distribution fixed, we next specify the model families used for two parts of the study: (i) measuring multimodal arithmetic accuracy at scale and (ii) running token-level probes and adaptations that require access to cross-entropy losses. The broader multimodal scaling analysis evaluates Gemini, Qwen, OpenAI, and xAI model families. The forced-completion, behavioral nudge, and adapter-geometry analyses focus on Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B-Instruct and Qwen3-VL235B-A22B-Instruct, which provide the token-loss access needed for those experiments. We report the Qwen results side by side (30B/235B) in the probing, while the multimodal accuracy curves summarize the broader evaluation setting.

sections.title: 3.3 Multimodal Evaluation
sections.content: To isolate modality effects, we evaluate the same underlying multiplication items while varying only the input channel. The paired benchmark contains 10,000 shared multiplication instances, each rendered as text, an image, and (where possible) audio. The regression figures also include broader representation variants, including alphabetic text and rendered alphabetic prompts. With the paired multimodal instances now in hand, we next quantify degradation with increasing difficulty by fitting, separately per modality, logistic regression that predicts correctness in problem from its arithmetic load: (correcti) = σ(β0 + β1 Loadi), (2) where σ is the logistic function. This formulation highlights arithmetic load as scalar predictor comparable across problem families. Error-rate model. If each primitive digit operation fails independently with probability p, then (correcti) = (1 p)Nops exp(pNops), so logaccuracy is linear in the operation count (coarsely proxied by C). We use logistic regression as convenient monotone fit across modalities; its slope (β1) should be read such that higher (less negative) values indicate slower degradation as arithmetic load increases, and its intercept (β0) such that higher value indicates stronger baseline performance (see Table 3). log link on (correcti) matches the independent-error approximation; other monotone links (including log-log) could be reasonable under different parameterizations of Nops (see B).

sections.title: 3.4 Heuristic Fingerprinting
sections.content: Accuracy-versus-load curves tell us when models break down, but they do not by themselves reveal which intermediate procedure the model is predisposed to follow on given instance. This is important because it will give insight into whether the observed failures arise from uniform loss of arithmetic competence or from shifts in the models preferred computation strategy across problems and modalities. Indeed, DD can be efficient for clean place-value structures, such as 47 60; RC can be efficient for near-base problems, such as 49 51; OT can be helpful for unstructured operands. Thus, two mathematically similar problems can become easier or harder depending on the heuristic chosen. The fingerprinting probe is designed to measure these compatibility differences across modalities in controlled way. To probe which procedural strategy model finds most natural for given multiplication instance (Belinkov, 2022), we introduce perplexitystyle loss probe implemented with Qwen3-VL-30BA3B-Instruct and Qwen3-VL-235B-A22B. We infer heuristic preference from relative loss under forced continuation (token-level cross-entropy), enabling large-scale preference measurement without confounds from sampling, verbosity, or post-hoc answer formatting. For each problem (a, b), we construct small bank of heuristic-conditioned assistant continuations, each describing specific computation style in short, operand-free language so that the same template bank can be applied to matched text and image presentations: OT: Columnar: start with the ones digits [...] DD: Decomposition: split one factor into placevalue parts [...] RC: Round & adjust: use nearby round base, then compensate [...] To ensure that loss differences reflect genuine heuristic alignment, the probe uses balanced bank of short, stylistically matched paraphrases per heuristic, together with neutral baseline preamble, so that we can report relative preferences as loss rather than raw loss. We score each heuristic by the length-normalized cross-entropy of its continuation tokens under the same question context (forced completion), averaging across the active template bank, and we interpret lower loss (or more negative loss relative to the neutral prompt) as deeper alignment with that heuristics initial reasoning trajectory. We treat the minimum-loss aggregated template bank as per-instance, loss-based label and analyze how both labels and margins shift with operand cues and modality; we compute loss only on the heuristic preamble/continuation tokens. Likelihood-ratio view. Framing the probe as likelihood ratio will provide formal statistical justification for interpreting cross-entropy differences as the models explicit algorithmic preference. Let ℓ(h) be the length-normalized crossentropy over the heuristic template continuation tokens under the same question context (forced completion), and let ℓ0 be the neutral baseline. If templates are token-length matched with length , then ℓ(h) equals the log-likelihood ratio between candidate templates under the same model and context. Then, choosing the minimum loss heuristic is the maximum-likelihood choice under equal priors. When lengths differ, ℓ should be read as normalized naturalness score (see B). This setup enables cross-modal heuristic comparison. We evaluate the probe on two components of our benchmark: first, the Heuristic-Disagreement Set (HDS), which contains 1000 problems intentionally chosen so that their operand configurations distinctively favor one of the three target heuristics, such as near-base numbers where RC is attractive. Items are labeled by an OT/DD/RC cost model. Each item stores both design family and target heuristic, defined as the minimum-cost heuristic under that cost model. We enforce uniqueness up to commutativity and split HDS into train/validation/test using 70/15/15. We define simple cost models for OT/DD/RC (see B). We keep only instances where the minimum-cost heuristic is uniquely separated from the runner-up by margin. Table 2 shows small snippet of HDS entries. ID hds_000 hds_002 hds_009 hds_014 hds_018 hds_019 Target Category Problem 49 51 = 2499 RC 99 101 = 9999 RC 47 60 = 2820 DD 37 100 = 3700 DD 87 96 = 8352 OT 79 68 = 5372 OT near_50_sym near_100_sym zero_factor hundred_factor carry_heavy carry_heavy Table 2: Snippet of HDS examples showing operands, target heuristics, and category labels. Full data are available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ cjerzak/MultimodalMathBenchmarks. Separately, we include Adversarial Traps. These traps deliberately make familiar cue unreliable: for example, an anti-rounding trap makes RC look tempting while an offset creates an extra correction burden; missing-term trap tests whether decomposition trace drops required partial product. Performance here tests whether heuristic preferences remain robust to misleading cues.

sections.title: 3.5 Adapter Weight Orthogonality Analysis
sections.content: Because loss-based preferences could, in principle, reflect trace-format effects, we additionally connect differences in strategy to the parameter space. To test whether different heuristics correspond to meaningfully distinct computational mechanisms, we train separate LoRA adapters (Hu et al., 2022) for Qwen3-VL-30B and Qwen3VL-235B using synthetic reasoning traces generated with no overlap with the held-out test problems. For each model, we train three heuristic adapters (RC, DD, OT) plus STYLE control adapter trained on generic reasoning-trace formatting without heuristic-specific intermediate arithmetic. The three heuristic adapters drive the main nudge and pairwise geometry analyses, while STYLE serves as control for whether any degradation is heuristic-specific or broader trace-steering effect. We train the heuristic adapters with early stopping on validation loss1, keeping the base model fixed and learning only low-rank updates. For each adapter, we compute the effective update Wh = BhAh across all LoRA modules and flatten Wh into vector. We then compute the pairwise cosine similarity between the update vectors of any two heuristic adapters h1 and h2: sim(h1, h2) = Wh1 Wh2 Wh1Wh2 . (3) As is invariant to the LoRA factorization, sim(h1, h2) provides an invariant proxy for separability that complements behavioral probing, linking heuristic behavior to parameter-space structure.

sections.title: 4 Results
sections.content: With the benchmark, modalities, and probing setup defined, we now report empirical results and use consistent notation when comparing models. Unless otherwise noted, we report paired Qwen3-VL values as 30B / 235B, each with standard errors (SE) across problem instances.

sections.title: Across Modalities
sections.content: We begin by establishing baseline for how structural difficulty affects performance, addressing our first research question on algorithmic load. Figure 2 shows logistic regression curves for the probability of correct answer versus arithmetic load across modalities. Accuracy declines monotonically with load across modalities, but the ordering depends on representation and model. Overall, text is usually the strongest; audio and image variants tend to lag: curves approach near-zero accuracy at the highest observed difficulty levels. Non-text conditions often have lower intercepts, though fitted slopes are not consistently steeper. Some models (e.g., Gemini 2.5 Flash) show similar scaling performance with across modalities, 1For 30B: RC = 845 train, 155 val; DD = 852 train, 148 val; OT = 830 train, 170 val; for 235B: RC = 845 train, 155 val; DD = 852 train, 148 val; OT = 830 train, 170 val. while others show greater apparent sensitivity (such as GPT 5.4 or Grok 4.20). The fitted coefficients in Table 3 make these visual trends more explicit. Across modelmodality pairs, arithmetic load remains strong onedimensional summary of performance: most fits explain roughly half or more of the outcome variation, with R2 values commonly above 0.5. The 50% load threshold is especially useful for comparing models because it combines the intercept and slope into single difficulty scale. Gemini 2.5 Flash, Qwen3-VL-30B, and GPT-4o all tend to cross 50% predicted accuracy near 5054, whereas Qwen3-VL-235B remains substantially more robust, with 50% thresholds near 74 75 across its text and image conditions. GPT5.4 and Grok 4.20 occupy higher-performing but more modality-sensitive regime: both retain relatively large 50% thresholds in numerical text or numerical-image settings, but their alphabeticimage conditions drop noticeably. The table also shows why slopes alone should not be over-interpreted. Some models with steep negative slopes, such as GPT-5.4, nevertheless perform well over broad range because they begin from very high intercept, while flatter slopes paired with lower intercepts can still yield lower accuracy at moderate loads. Finally, the modality effects are more often expressed as shifts in intercept or 50% threshold than as uniformly steeper degradation: numerical images often track numerical text closely, alphabetic images are the most consistent weak point, and audio does not show uniform penalty in the controlled conditions where it was evaluated. Taken together, these summaries support the main claim that arithmetic load is the dominant axis of degradation, while modality and representation mainly modulate where along that axis each model begins to fail.

sections.title: 4.2 Decomposition Preference?
sections.content: Having established that arithmetic load predicts degradation across modalities, we next ask whether models exhibit preferences for certain arithmetic procedures that might help explain these failures. To test whether LLMs tend to prefer any single heuristic, we compare how well heuristic-specific preambles match the models continuation distribution via forced-completion loss probe. Table 4 reports mean loss relative to the neutral baseline on shared held-out split (values < 0 indicate lower forced-completion loss than neutral). ), Qwen3-VL-30B ( ), and xAIs Grok 4.20 ( Figure 2: Probability of correct answer as function of arithmetic load (total digits non-zero digits) across input modalities for Gemini 2.5 Flash ( ), 5.4 ( ) models. Curves show fitted logistic regression models for numeric/alphabetic text, image renderings of those prompts, and audio (where possible).Note: Gemini 3.1 Pro performed flawlessly across all modalities within the range of in the original evaluation suite (1-100), but also took over an hour per modality (as opposed to minutes for other models) and used far more output tokens than other models (by factor of at least 10). Further trials were therefore run over larger range of (1-400). In this extended analysis, Gemini 3.1 Pro began to degrade at value of around 360, which occurs, for example, in problem like: 1632178320 5683473970 = ?. ), and Qwen3-VL-235B ( ), with OpenAIs 4o ( Here, target support is the normalized exp(ℓ) mass assigned to the target heuristic among DD, RC, OT, and the neutral baseline. Because the neutral continuation is included as competitor, we do not force heuristic winner when all heuristic cues score worse than neutral. For both 30B and 235B, DD attains the lowest loss in both text and image modalities. For 30B, DD attains the most negative loss in text and the smallest positive loss in image (-0.5060 / +0.9156), with RC and OT less favorable. For 235B, DD again shows the smallest (least positive) loss in both modalities (+1.4734 / +1.4594), while OT is the least compatible continuation (+2.3409 / +2.2722). The support statistics are likewise DDdominant across both sizes: 30B places among the largest resolved-probe target support on DDtargeted items (27.9% 0.3% / 22.3% 0.3%), as does 235B (15.2%0.2% / 18.7%0.4%). Raw cross-entropy losses with SE and Traps comparisons are reported in C.2. style-shift ablation suggests that the loss-based fingerprint is not just memorizing familiar wording. If the probe were mostly doing surface-form matching, then replacing the balanced template bank with style-shift paraphrases should broadly collapse the aggregate design-family match rate. Yet, heuristic-match moves by only few points in every setting: 34.0% to 31.2% in 30B text, 33.3% to 34.0% in 30B image, 28.5% to 30.6% in 235B text, and 30.6% to 32.6% in 235B image. Accuracy likewise stays in the same broad range rather than collapsing (49.3% to 50.7%, 46.5% to 46.5%, 57.6% to 64.6%, and 53.5% to 50.0%). The clearest effect of the mismatch is increased noise: the mean within-problem standard deviation across paraphrase losses rises from 0.3928 to 0.7136, from 0.3821 to 0.6649, from 0.3987 to 0.7122, and from 0.4516 to 0.6663  (Table 9)  . In that sense, changing template style acts more like changing an accent than changing the underlying arithmetic cue: the readout gets noisier, but it usually still points to the same family. That said, some family-specific rates are reweighted, especially in 30B image, where DD-target detection falls. Overall, the ablation shows that stylistic perturbations can compress or reweight margins, but they do not usually erase the fingerprint signal. Contrastive step probe. Table 3: Results across models and modalities. Higher β0 indicates stronger baseline performance; higher (less negative) β1 indicates slower degradation as arithmetic load increases; 50% indicates the load at which predicted accuracy is reduced to 50% (higher is better). The R2 column indicates the fraction of outcome variability explained by the fit. For an interactive leaderboard, see: https://neuristemic.ai/multiplication-in-multimodal-llms/. the possibility that these preferences merely reflect superficial stylistic biases over actual computational grounding. Therefore, to test deeper method grounding, we introduce contrastive step probe. Here, we score correct step versus plausible incorrect intermediate step that is matched in form for each heuristic. Table 5 illustrates. Most correct-step preferences are at ceiling (100%) on target-aligned HDS items, so the probe primarily confirms basic grounding. The nontrivial signal comes from the loss gap between correct and incorrect. Although overall preference rates differ little by modality (text overall: 100.0% / 100.0%; image overall: 97.9% / 100.0%), they have mean loss gaps of +0.2648 / +0.8804 (text) and +0.2119 / +0.2752 (image) for correct and incorrect steps. Margins vary by heuristic, with loss-gap patterns varying also by model and modality (text OT: +0.5538 / +0.6141 vs DD: +0.1209 / +1.9840; image OT: +0.4856 / +0.5918 vs DD: +0.0652 / +0.0901). Table 10 summarizes. These modality differences suggest that preferred arithmetic continuations are determined not solely by the mathematical structure of the problem but also at least to some extent by how the problem is presented. One possible explanation is that rendered equations change the salience of magnitude information (Dehaene, 2011), although our results cannot directly test this mechanism. See E.1 for an analysis of adversarial cases.

sections.title: Improve Performance
sections.content: Given that the probes reveal stable heuristic preferences, we next ask whether we can actively steer strategy selection with lightweight adaptation. Specifically, for each base model (Qwen3VL-30B and Qwen3-VL-235B), we first trained three separate LoRA adapters on approximately 1,000 synthetic heuristic-specific reasoning traces. These traces are generated programmatically with no overlap with the held-out HDS/Traps evaluation problems: an RC adapter on near-base rounding/compensation examples, DD adapter on decomposition-friendly partial-product examples, and an OT adapter on carry-heavy column-style examples. These adapters were trained with early stopping on validation split. We then evaluated the resulting adapters on the held-out HDS test split (n = 144 / 144) to ask whether forcing one trace family at inference time Metric Accuracy (Mean = 177.4) Model 30B 235B Text Image 49.3% 4.2% 46.5% 4.2% 57.6% 4.1% 53.5% 4.2% Neutral baseline loss: Neutral baseline 30B 235B DD (Decomposition) 7.5265 4.9058 loss vs. neutral under forced completion (lower = deeper alignment): -0.5060 +1.4734 -0.4441 +1.8791 -0.4398 +2.3409 30B 235B 30B 235B 30B 235B OT (Columnar) RC (Rounding) 4.5022 4.1863 +0.9156 +1.4594 +1.6518 +2.2114 +0.9776 +2.2722 Target support on resolved target problems: DD preference share (on DD-targeted items) RC preference share (on RC-targeted items) OT preference share (on OT-targeted items) 30B 235B 30B 235B 30B 235B 27.9% 0.3% 22.3% 0.3% 15.2% 0.2% 18.7% 0.4% 26.5% 0.5% 10.5% 0.3% 12.4% 0.3% 8.8% 0.3% 25.8% 0.4% 17.8% 0.4% 6.8% 0.3% 5.9% 0.3% Table 4: Modality comparison on shared held-out probe split (n = 144) for Qwen3-VL-30B and Qwen3-VL-235B. Raw cross-entropy losses are reported in C.2. Only arithmetic correctness changes; wording and local format are held fixed. 1. Fix the same problem One HDS item with the same operands (a, b), shown either as text or as rendered image. Example: 47 36 2. Build matched step pair Choose one heuristic (OT/DD/RC), then write two candidate next steps with identical wording. Correct: 40 36 + 7 36 = 1440 + 252 Incorrect: 40 36 + 7 36 = 1440 + 262 3. Score both continuations Keep the prompt context fixed and compute forcedcompletion loss on the step tokens only: ℓcorrect and ℓincorrect 4. Aggregate the comparison Preference event: ℓcorrect < ℓincorrect Report both overall and separately on OT-, DD-, and RC-targeted items. Loss gap: ℓincorrect ℓcorrect Table 5: Contrastive step probe for text and image inputs, where we compare correct and plausible incorrect intermediate step with matched surface form; lower forced-completion loss indicates which step is more compatible with the models continuation distribution. improves arithmetic behavior out of distribution. Applying the 3 / 3 trained heuristic LoRAs (RC, DD, OT) produced 114 / 121 behavioral (correctness) flips across 432 / 432 comparisons (3 / 3 LoRAs 144 / 144 problems per model)1 / 4 improved and 113 / 117 degradedand 313 / 291 heuristic flips (changes in the lowest-loss template). Degradations tend to be more prevalent than improvements for both model sizes. post-hoc answer analysis shows recurring partial-product omissions; magnitude slips and carry-drop errors were not observed (12, 0, 0 for 30B; 8, 0, 0 for 235B). These results suggest that while LoRAs successfully learn heuristic-specific reasoning patterns (as evidenced by low validation loss), the models internal heuristic router is better optimized for performance than any single heuristic; internal preferences appear to handle arithmetic reasoning better than any single enforced trace distribution. To isolate whether observed degradations stem from heuristic specialization or from more general disruption of the base models native arithmetic routing by synthetic traces, we also evaluated style-only control adapter trained solely to imitate reasoning-trace formatting without any heuristicspecific arithmetic content. Here, the STYLE control yields almost exclusively degraded cases. In other words, even an adapter trained to imitate reasoning-trace format without imposing heuristicspecific arithmetic content still reduces correctness much more often than it helps. This suggests that the degradation seen under heuristic LoRAs is not just consequence of specializing to OT, DD, or RC in particular; more broadly, pushing the model toward synthetic trace distribution appears to disrupt the base models native routing over arithmetic behaviors, at least in the training regime here.

sections.title: 4.3.1 Heuristics in Update Space
sections.content: Finally, Table 8 shows the cosine similarity between the effective LoRA update matrices for the three heuristic adapters; values are near zero. In sensitivity analysis, we retrain adapters for the same heuristic using different random seeds. Sameheuristic reruns show higher cosine similarity than cross-heuristic pairs (0.2553 vs. 0.1055 with gap 0.1498 for 30B; 0.1476 vs. 0.0447 with gap 0.1029 for 235B), suggesting that the observed heuristic separation in parameter space is not primarily high-dimensional artifact of LoRA training. Heuristic fine-tuning does not seem to trigger very similar updates in parameter space.

sections.title: 5 Limitations
sections.content: Overall, our results show that exact multiplication performance in LLMs is governed by the computational burden induced by digit structure, secondarily modulated by input modality. Across text, image, and audio renderings, accuracy declines sharply with arithmetic load; forcedcompletion probes further suggest preference for decomposition-style continuations whose margins shift under non-text modalities. Notwithstanding these results, our study has several limitations. First, the task scope is narrow: we focus on multiplication, so patterns may differ for addition, division, symbolic algebra, or multi-step word problems. Second, model coverage remains limited. The multimodal accuracy analysis covers several model families  (Table 3)  , but token-level fingerprinting and LoRA nudges are limited to Qwen3-VL-30B and Qwen3-VL-235B. Third, we rely on synthetic digit templates that may not match the distributions of real-world mathematical problems. The forced-preamble technique measures compatibility with heuristic-conditioned continuations, rather than direct readout of the models internal algorithm. Finally, our multimodal conditions use controlled renderings and do not encompass the full range of messy real-world inputs. This design is useful for isolating arithmetic structure and modality effects, but future work should test scanned documents, handwritten equations, spreadsheet screenshots, and tool-augmented agent settings. Regarding risks, while our benchmark and probing methodology are intended only for diagnostic characterization, the same forced-completion loss and LoRA-based techniques could in principle be repurposed to systematically map and exploit arithmetic failure modes in deployed multimodal systems, underscoring the importance of robust adversarial testing and verifiable computational safeguards.

sections.title: Evaluation Protocol
sections.content: Decoding parameters. All generations in the LoRA pipeline use temperature = 0 (deterministic). The maximum generation budget is 2048 tokens. LoRA learning rate and other hyper-parameters set using Tinker API defaults. We attempted to deploy an audio analysis on Qwen3-omni variant but experienced technical difficulties. Uncertainty estimates. Standard errors (SE) are computed across problem instances. For accuracy and resolved-probe coverage, we use the binomial SE (cid:112)p(1 p)/n (reported in percentage points). For loss averages and resolved-probe target support, we use the sample standard deviation n, with targetof per-problem values divided by support SE computed only across the resolved rows for that family. With deterministic decoding (temperature = 0), these SEs reflect item-level variability. HDS construction. We construct the HeuristicDisagreement Set (HDS) by seeding it with handcurated pool of problems that distinctively favor one of the three target heuristics. We then scale to target count by sampling additional items into three roughly equal buckets based on design families: RC draws offsets within 10 around bases {25, 50, 100, 200, 250, 500}; DD draws problems with trailing zeros, multiples of 25, or clean tens decompositions; and OT draws carry-heavy highdigit pairs or generic pairs explicitly away from near-base and zero cues. We also include small perturbation pairs that flip single cue (e.g., near-base vs. not) to test the sensitivity of heuristic detection. To formalize the target labels, each item is evaluated using cost proxy based on primitive digit operations. OT uses the schoolbook cost of digit multiplications plus small carry/accumulation penalty; DD uses one-sided expansion cost of min(ms, nt) plus additions for combining partial products, with explicit low-cost special cases for trailing-zero and quarter-hundred factors; and RC uses shared-base correction cost built from the nearest round base and the two operand offsets. Items are retained in the final dataset only when the minimum-cost heuristic is uniquely separated from the runner-up by fixed margin. Finally, we enforce problem uniqueness up to commutativity, generate adversarial traps disjoint from the HDS, and stratify train/val/test splits using 70/15/15 to preserve the intended target mix. AI use statement. AI was used for code correctness and grammar review.

sections.title: B Notes on Operation Counts and Probes
sections.content: Operation-count proxy. Let and be positive integers with digit lengths n, and non-zero digit counts s, t, respectively, so 1 and 1 m. full digit expansion contains exactly st non-zero digit products. If these shifted product terms are summed sequentially, this requires at most st 1 term additions, not counting the lowerlevel digit additions and carry propagation inside each addition. one-sided digit expansion that expands the non-zero digits of against all digits of uses ms digit multiplications, including multiplications by zero; exactly st of these products are non-zero. Similarly, expanding against uses nt digit multiplications. Now, the proxy = (n + m)(s + t) is monotone in total digit length and total non-zero digit count. It directly upper-bounds ms and nt, since both appear as nonnegative terms in the expansion: = ns + nt + ms + mt. It also satisfies (by the arithmetic mean-geometric mean inequality): st (s + t)2 4 (n + m)(s + t) 4 = , where the second inequality uses + + m. Thus, C/4 upper-bounds the number of nonzero pairwise digit products. is therefore only proxy for intermediate computation number: it ignores carry propagation, aggregation order, and heuristic-specific shortcuts, and therefore does not, in general, preserve the exact ordering of operation counts across problems. and tokens template be ℓ(h) Likelihood-ratio probe. Let xh the = heuristic (cid:80)Th 1 t=1 log p(xh,t c) the length-normalized Th cross-entropy under the same base model for context (the multiplication problem), computed on forced continuation tokens. If templates are token-length matched (Th = ), then ℓ(h) equals the log-likelihood ratio log p(xhc) p(x0c) between candidate templates under the same model. Selecting the minimum-loss heuristic is therefore the maximum-likelihood choice among candidates under equal priors; when token lengths differ, ℓ is normalized naturalness score rather than an exact decision rule. Forced-Completion Probe Details and

sections.title: Results
sections.content: C.1 Templates Our forced-completion loss probe uses heuristicspecific assistant continuations that are short and style-matched. The balanced template bank contains multiple paraphrases per heuristic; below, we show one representative continuation for each class: Columnar (OT): Column method: start with the ones digits [...] Decomposition (DD): Decomposition: split one factor into place-value parts [...] Rounding-Compensation (RC): Round and adjust: use nearby round base, then compensate [...] Neutral Baseline: Let me solve this multiplication problem step by step [...] We compute cross-entropy loss over the template continuation tokens under forced completion and report the difference (loss) relative to the neutral baseline. The active bank is intentionally short and stylistically matched but not strictly token-lengthidentical, so we use length-normalized loss to mitigate residual token-length differences. C.2 Forced-Completion Loss Results For completeness, we report raw cross-entropy losses (with SE) under forced completion and the original modality comparison table. These absolute losses underlie the loss summaries in the main text. Heuristic DD (Decomposition) RC (Rounding) OT (Columnar) 30B/235B Avg. Loss SE 7.02 0.02 / 6.38 0.01 7.08 0.01 / 6.78 0.01 7.09 0.01 / 7.25 0.01 Table 6: Raw cross-entropy loss by heuristic template on the held-out probe split (n = 144). Lower values indicate lower forced-completion loss (a deeper continuation preference).

sections.title: D Representative Analysis Examples
sections.content: D.1 LoRA Training Examples We trained heuristic-specific LoRA adapters using synthetic reasoning traces. Below are representative examples showing the expected reasoning pattern for each heuristic. These traces were generated programmatically to ensure consistent structure while varying the specific realizations. Trace generation. Each heuristic uses distinct algorithmic template: RC: For symmetric pairs (a = base + k, = base k), applies the difference of squares identity (a)(b) = base2 k2. Non-symmetric pairs use general rounding approach with four adjustment terms. DD: Decomposes the larger operand into tens and ones components, computes two partial products via the distributive property, and sums them. OT: Simulates columnar multiplication with explicit digit-by-digit products, carries, and partial product accumulation. Problem selection. Problems were selected to match each heuristics strengths, using programmatic generators used in the analysis. RC examples draw from bases {25, 50, 75, 100, 125, 150, 175, 200, 250, 300, 400, 500} with offsets 15, including both symmetric and mixed near-base pairs. DD examples mix trailing-zero factors, easy tensplus-ones decompositions, and generic two-digit pairs. OT examples mix carry-heavy 23 digit pairs with generic multi-digit numbers. The generator also supports STYLE control dataset with generic trace formatting but no heuristic-specific intermediate computation. To ensure unbiased evaluation, we exclude all HDS validation/test problems, Traps problems, and held-out multimodal problems from training data. Split DD RC OT Lowest-loss heuristic HDS (Test) Traps 7.02 0.02 7.42 0.02 7.08 0.01 7.14 0.05 7.09 0.01 7.30 0.02 DD RC Table 7: Results for Qwen3-VL-30B-A3B. Entries are average forced-completion loss SE; lower values indicate deeper alignment. Rounding-Compensation (RC) Example PROBLEM: 399 399 = 159201 What is 399 399? Let me round to convenient bases and adjust . 399 is close to 400 ( difference : -1) . 399 is close to 400 ( difference : -1) . Start with 400 400 = 160000. Adjustment for 399: 400 -1 = -400. Adjustment for 399: -1 400 = -400. Cross term : -1 -1 = +1. Total : 160000 + ( -400) + ( -400) + (+1) = 159201. Answer : Decomposition (DD) Example PROBLEM: 99 40 = 3960 What is 99 40? Let me decompose 99 into 90 + 9. First compute 90 40: 90 40 = 3600. Then compute 9 40: 9 40 = 360. Now sum the partial products : 3600 + 360 = 3960. Answer : 3960 Ones-Then-Tens (OT) Example PROBLEM: 79 78 = 6162 What is 79 78? Let me use column multiplication step by step . Step 1: Multiply 79 by ones digit 8: 9 8 = 72 , write 2, carry 7. 7 8 = 56 , plus carry = 63. First partial product : 632. Step 2: Multiply 79 by tens digit 7: 9 7 = 63 , write 3, carry 6. 7 7 = 49 , plus carry = 55. Second partial product : 553 ( shifted by 10 = 5530) . Step 3: Add partial products : 632 + 5530 = 6162. OT and DD updates are nearly orthogonal (ρ = 0.0726 / 0.0412), suggesting the model recruits distinct parameter subspaces to implement these strategies. E.1 Adversarial Traps To examine robustness in adversarial cases, we also study adversarial traps: test-only problems crafted to target known weaknesses of specific heuristics, serving as focused probe of strategy robustness In our trap set (n = beyond the HDS setting. 30), the analysis contributes direct comparison of heuristic preference signals under adversarial pressure, clarifying which strategies degrade and which remain stable relative to the held-out probe split. When presented with anti-round traps, the smaller 30B model shifts toward RC: its RC targetsupport share rises from 26.5% 0.5% on the standard held-out split to 34.5% 1.4% on the traps. The 235B model also increases on RCtargeted items, but much more modestly, moving from 12.4% 0.3% to 15.6% 0.3%. We observe different dynamics with other strategies: DD target support moves from 27.9% 0.3% to 24.1% 0.4% for 30B and from 15.2% 0.2% to 19.3% 0.2% for 235B on missing-term traps, while OT target support moves from 25.8% 0.4% to 31.7% 1.4% for 30B and from 5.9% 0.3% to 12.1% 0.3% for 235B on OT-targeted traps. These results indicate that adversarial operand cues redistribute heuristic preference mass, with the strongest RC-specific shift appearing in the smaller model.
